text: To realize
To rush
To take off
To break down
To line up
To go off
Stuck
Traffic jam
Relieved
On time
I was waiting at the gate when they announced the delay.
We were having dinner when the alarm went off.
The agent was checking my passport when I realized my mistake.
I was sleeping when the plane landed.
I was waiting at the gate when the screen changed.
All of a sudden, the taxi broke down.
At that point, I was still calm.
I could not believe it.
The driver was looking at the engine, and I was looking at my watch. I could not believe it.
The facts were never the problem. The background was missing.
Two hours. Two more hours in this airport. Sorry, I am talking to myself. It has been a very long day.
A long day? I have two hours too. What were you doing this morning?
This morning? At six o'clock I was sleeping in a very nice hotel in Porto. My alarm was set for six, and it did not go off. I still do not know why.
Oh no. And then? What happened when you woke up?
The sun woke me up. The sun! I looked at my phone and it was ten past eight, and my train was at nine. I was rushing around that room like a crazy man.
Did you catch the train?

I did! I was very proud of myself. And then we were sitting on that train for forty minutes, in the middle of nowhere, and nobody was saying anything. All of a sudden a man came and told us: the train in front of us broke down.
So you were stuck on the train. What were you thinking at that point?

Honestly? I was thinking about my daughter. It is her wedding on Saturday, in London, and I was sitting in a dead train in Portugal. I arrived here at two o'clock, I was lining up for three hours, and they put me on the eight o'clock flight. I could not believe it, but I am going to be there.
Listen to me, talking and talking. And you? Two hours here as well: what happened to you today?

Me? Nothing like your day. I was having a coffee downstairs when they changed my gate, that is all. But your daughter is getting married on Saturday, and you are going to walk in on time. That is a good story, Martin.
All of a sudden, the taxi broke down and we were stuck.
At that point I realized my passport was in the other bag.
We were rushing to the gate, and I could not believe it.
In the end the next flight took off on time, and I was so relieved.

So you want to hear about the worst day of my trip? All right. My name is Frank, and this happened to me in Rome, two years ago, and my wife still tells this story at every dinner party we go to. Our flight was at eleven in the morning. Eleven. We had a whole morning, and I was not worried at all. At seven o'clock I was sleeping like a baby, and my alarm did not go off. It did not go off because I set it for seven in the evening. Seven P M. So at half past eight my wife was shaking my shoulder and shouting my name, and that is how the day started. We were running to the taxi at a quarter to nine, and we were laughing, because we still had two hours, and two hours is a lot. Then we got on the highway, and we stopped. A traffic jam. A big one. We were sitting in that taxi for fifty minutes, and we were not moving one meter. At that point I was still calm. Then, all of a sudden, the engine made a noise like a sick animal, and the taxi broke down. Right there, in the middle of the traffic jam. The driver was looking at the engine and I was looking at my watch, and it was twenty past ten. I could not believe it. We took our bags and we walked. We walked with two suitcases on the side of a highway in Rome, and a bus stopped for us, and I am still thankful to that driver. At the airport, I was lining up at the desk when I realized my passport was in the front pocket of the other suitcase. The one my wife was carrying. We were rushing to the gate when the woman there closed the door. She closed it, and she looked at me, and she said, I am sorry, sir. And through the window we were watching our plane take off. Without us. So, what did I learn? Two things. Read your alarm, morning or evening. And leave early. That is all. Now everybody has heard this story a hundred times, and my wife tells it better than me.

Hello. My name is Teresa, I am seventy-one years old, and I want to tell you about a train station in Italy, because I think you need to hear this one. I started traveling alone when I was sixty-six. Before that, never. My husband did the talking for forty years, and then my husband was gone, and I thought, well, that is the end of Italy for me. It was not. So. Two years ago I was standing in the station in Florence, and I was waiting for the train to Bologna, and I was very proud of myself, because I was doing all of it alone. And then a voice said something in Italian, and everybody around me stood up and started walking away. Everybody. And I was sitting there with my little suitcase, and I did not understand one word. My train was cancelled. I found that out three minutes later, from a screen. Now, this is the part I want you to hear. The old me, the one with the husband, would have cried. Really. But I did not cry. I walked to the information desk, and there was a long line, and I lined up, and I waited twenty minutes, and I said the only thing I had: my train is cancelled, what should I do? Six words. That is all I had. And the young man behind the desk, he answered me slowly, because he saw my face, and he put me on a train forty minutes later, and he wrote the platform number on a piece of paper for me. Fourteen. I still have that piece of paper at home, in a drawer. And when I was sitting on that train to Bologna, I was looking out of the window, and I was crying a little bit then, but for the other reason. Because I did it. Nobody did it for me. So here is what I want to say to you. You are going to have a bad day. The train is going to be cancelled, and the alarm is not going to go off, and something is going to break down. That is not the problem. The problem is thinking that you need perfect English to survive a bad day. You do not. I had six words, and they were enough. Go.

On time
I was waiting at the gate when they announced the delay.
We were having dinner when the alarm went off.
It was seven in the morning and the room was silent. My alarm did not go off.
We were sitting in a traffic jam when the taxi broke down.
I was lining up at the desk when I realized my passport was in the other bag.
Fifty minutes in the same place. We were stuck.
They gave me a seat on the next flight. I was so relieved.
The door was already closed, and our plane was taking off. I could not believe it.
All of a sudden, my alarm did not go off and I was still sleeping.
At that point I realized my passport was in the other bag.
We were rushing to the gate, and I could not believe it.
All of a sudden, the taxi broke down and we were stuck.
In the end the next flight took off on time, and I was so relieved.
